ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 covers spring roll as a general category; no Thai-specific crispy fried variant code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers spring rolls generically; no Thai crispy deep-fried variant subcode identified. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for spring roll or Thai fried pastry roll.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 4 crispy spring rolls, each approximately 50 g, totalling around 200 g as typically served at a Thai restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Spring Roll, Fried (Por Pia Tod) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table analogue; values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 35173 (egg roll / spring roll, fried)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy); FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table 2nd edition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS entry for Thai crispy spring roll (Por Pia Tod) was identified. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried spring roll / egg roll (NDB 35173, ~230 kcal/100g) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA fried spring roll analogues. Per-100g values reflect a deep-fried wheat-pastry roll with mixed vegetable and minced meat filling, consistent with Thai Por Pia Tod preparation. Serving size set at 200 g (4 × ~50 g rolls).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Spring Roll / Fried Pastry Roll — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for spring roll and fried wheat-pastry foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Thai crispy spring roll (Por Pia Tod) was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: fried spring roll / egg roll with wheat pastry wrapper and mixed filling, which falls in the low-to-medium GI range (GI ~50–55) per published University of Sydney GI Database data for similar fried pastry rolls. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 50.0) / 100 = 26.0, where carbohydrate per 200 g serving = 25.0 g/100g × 2 = 50.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg/100g; ~760 mg per 4-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Thai crispy spring rolls, as a 4-piece serving provides approximately 760 mg sodium — over 30% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — primarily from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoned filling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~52) and high glycemic load per serving (~26) due to refined wheat pastry wrapper and carbohydrate-dense filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Thai crispy spring rolls in small portions, as the fried wheat-pastry wrapper contributes a glycemic load of approximately 26 per 4-piece serving, which can cause a meaningful postprandial blood glucose rise; pairing with non-starchy vegetables and limiting to 1–2 pieces is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (12 g/100g) and saturated fat (2.8 g/100g) from deep-frying in vegetable oil, combined with elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Thai crispy spring rolls should be consumed occasionally and in small portions, as deep-frying contributes moderate saturated fat and the high sodium content (~760 mg per serving) may adversely affect blood pressure and cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Crispy Spring Roll (Por Pia Tod / ปอเปี๊ยะทอด) is a popular Thai deep-fried snack consisting of a thin, crispy wheat-flour pastry wrapper encasing a savoury filling of glass noodles, shredded cabbage, carrots, mushrooms, and minced pork or chicken, seasoned with garlic, soy sauce, and oyster sauce. The rolls are deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and shatteringly crisp on the outside, with a moist, flavourful filling inside. They are typically served in sets of 4 pieces and accompanied by a sweet chilli dipping sauce. In Singapore, Thai crispy spring rolls are widely available at Thai restaurants and food delivery platforms such as field survey, where they are a common starter or snack item. Nutritionally, a 4-piece serving (~200 g) provides approximately 460 kcal, 13 g protein, 24 g fat, and 50 g carbohydrate, with a notable sodium content of ~760 mg. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate-to-high (~26) due to the refined pastry wrapper. Clinically, the dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate GI but high GL per serving), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and sodium). The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or shellfish allergies. It is also known as 炸春卷 in Chinese and Por Pia Tod or Por Pia Thod in romanized Thai.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry for Thai crispy spring roll (Por Pia Tod) was identified. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogue (fried spring roll/egg roll) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA fried pastry roll data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar fried wheat-pastry rolls. Confidence is medium because the source match is adapted rather than exact.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (fried spring roll/egg roll analogue) and FAO/INFOODS SEA; no exact Thai crispy spring roll entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source database for this food category. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source database for this food category. 4. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for fried spring rolls with wheat pastry wrappers; no direct GI measurement for Por Pia Tod was found. 5. Glycemic load per serving = (52 × 50.0 g carbohydrate per 200 g serving) / 100 = 26.0. 6. Halal status is marked false as oyster sauce is typically present and pork may be used; halal-certified variants may exist but cannot be confirmed for this specific restaurant record. 7. The food_name has been normalised from the input menu string '102D Thai Crispy Spring Roll (4pcs) 炸春卷' to the canonical English form 'Thai Crispy Spring Roll (4 pieces)'. 8. This dish is Thai cuisine; mandatory Thai-script local name (ปอเปี๊ยะทอด) is provided per G6 multilingual rules.
